channel.title: The Fix by JB - Jonathan Barlow
channel.description: Insights on leadership, systems thinking, technology, and Detroit from Jonathan Barlow.

item.title: The Real Measure of a City Isn't GDP — It's Whether People Stay
item.description: Detroit's population decline masks a deeper truth about urban success. We measure the wrong metrics while the real indicator sits in plain sight.

item.title: Trust Is Shifting From Institutions to Operators
item.description: Government trust at 22%. But trust isn't disappearing — it's relocating to operators who deliver results.

item.title: The Real Gap in America Is Execution
item.description: Every city has a strategic plan. Most say the same things. The plans are fine. The execution systems don't exist.

item.title: Why Billions in Funding Aren't Producing Results
item.description: Follow the money. $2.6 trillion flows through nonprofits. 70% of programs disappear when funding ends. The money is there. The coordination isn't.

item.title: Programs Don't Scale — Systems Do
item.description: Programs have start dates and end dates. Systems have feedback loops. The question isn't "did the program work?" — it's "did the system change?"

item.title: Opportunity Isn't Missing — It's Not Organized
item.description: In every city there are grants, programs, accelerators, training, capital. The resources exist. What doesn't exist is a map.

item.title: We Don't Have a Youth Problem — We Have a Pathway Problem
item.description: 4.5 million young people not in school or working. The conversation focuses on behavior. But behavior follows design.

item.title: Why Families Are Carrying the Weight of Broken Systems
item.description: When every other system fails, families are the last safety net. That's not resilience — it's structural failure being absorbed by the people least equipped to carry it.

item.title: Childcare Is the Hidden Economic Infrastructure
item.description: Average childcare costs $10,853/year per child. 57% of parents have turned down work because of it. Childcare isn't a social issue — it's economic infrastructure.

item.title: The Housing Crisis Is a Coordination Crisis
item.description: Detroit has 70,000 vacant lots. The national narrative says we need more housing. The real issue isn't supply — it's coordination.

item.title: AI Is Exposing a Bigger Problem Than Job Loss
item.description: 300 million jobs globally could be affected. But AI isn't replacing strong systems — it's exposing fragile ones we've been hiding behind.

item.title: Training Programs Don't Create Careers — Systems Do
item.description: Only 37% of workforce trainees are employed in their trained field at one year. A training program is one node. A career requires six.

item.title: The Workforce System Isn't Failing — It's Misaligned
item.description: $3.6 billion spent on workforce development annually. 87% of employers still can't find talent. The system isn't failing — it was never aligned.

item.title: We Fund Startups. We Don't Build Businesses.
item.description: 5.5 million new business applications filed last year. Half will close within five years. We celebrate the launch but nobody builds the boring middle.

item.title: America Isn't Broke — It's Disconnected
item.description: GDP is up. Markets are up. Employment is technically strong. But 60% of Americans still live paycheck to paycheck. The problem isn't money — it's architecture.

item.title: Why Your Network Isn't Working
item.description: You collect connections but don't build systems around them. Your network fails because you treat it like a Rolodex instead of infrastructure.

item.title: Build for the Person Who Stays
item.description: Most urban projects build for the "ideal user" who doesn't exist. Real impact means building for the person who stayed through every crisis.

item.title: Why Every Crisis is an Innovation Opportunity
item.description: Three years ago, Detroit's water infrastructure failed. While others saw catastrophe, I saw a laboratory for building resilient urban systems.

item.title: Programs vs. Systems: Why Detroit Keeps Starting Over
item.description: Detroit doesn't have a program problem. It has a systems problem. Every year, new initiatives launch with press releases and ribbon cuttings, yet the same problems persist.

item.title: Most Leadership Advice Is Backwards
item.description: They tell you to think big and start small. I say think in systems and start with structure.
